# Stars I

## Our stellar neighborhood

Distances in astronomy are measured variously in terms of (kilo)meters, AUs, parallax angles, parsecs, lightyears , and eventually something called red-shift. Solar system sizes were convenient in AUs, but this scale is not useful for considerations of stars (\~parsecs/lightyears) and galaxies (\~Mpc/Mly). 

|  |  Centauri | Barnard | Luhman 16 | Sagittarius A | Large Magellanic Cloud | Small Magellanic Cloud | Andromeda (M31) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| parallax | 0.77’’ | 0.55’’ |  | — | — | — | —  |
| parsecs | 1.3 pc |  |  |  | 50 kpc | 62 kpc |  |
| lightyears |  |  | 6.5 ly | 25 kly |  |  | 2.5 Mly |

In this table, we are using **stellar parallax** for nearby stars only. (Why?) This is defined as the angle it is displaced relative to the fixed stars as seen by us when we are displaced by half the diameter of our orbit around the Sun (see figure). 

1. One **parsec** is defined to be the distance at which an object suffers a stellar parallax of one arcsecond. In other words, you look at a distant object’s position in terms of direction in space. Then you move over laterally (in any direction) by a distance of 1 AU from where you were in the first measurement and measure the position again. The new measurement will differ in direction from the first by some angle . When \=1'' is one arcsecond[^1], then the distance to the object is defined to be one “arcsecond of parallax” or “parsec”. We demonstrate this in the diagram at right in which the ellipse is the orbit of the Earth (seen obliquely) with the Sun at the center. We view a distant star from two points in our orbit separated by 6 months so that the base of the cone is as large as possible, in our case 2 AU by definition. (Make sure you understand this part.) Use this to show that one parsec 1.0 pc \=2.1105 AU. 

2. 1 AU \= 500 light-seconds. Use this to show that  1.0 pc= 3.3 ly=3.1 1013 km. 

3. Verify that at 0.77’’ of stellar parallax,  Centauri is 1.3 pc away, and calculate the rest of the missing entries to fill out the table.

4. Plan a model (you don’t have to build it) in which 1 AU corresponds to 1 m.   
   1. What is the size of the Sun? Name an object this size.  
   2. What is the size of the Earth? Name an object this size.  
   3. How large is the solar system in this model? Relate it to something in TJ. (Hint: The building is about 200 m across.)  
   4. How far is the closest star? Find a place that far away from TJ. 

This work should serve to give us an idea of the scales involved in our discussion of stars. For example, all the stars we see in the night sky are in our own galaxy (called the Milky Way). We are 8 kpc from the galactic center. The Milky Way is roughly 27 kpc in diameter and about 2.5-5 kpc thick.   
![][image1]![][image2]

Actually, on a good night, we can also see a few (dwarf) galaxies, the brightest and most famous of which is Andromeda. Andromeda is the nearest (non-dwarf) galaxy to the Milky Way, but it is a whopping 765 kpc away. That is roughly 36 times further than the stars at the far end of the galactic disk from us and 590 times the distance to the nearest star. 

5. In your model above, how far away is the galactic center?

A handful of stars are so large and bright that their angular size can be measured directly. Betelgeuse’s angular size is \=0.045'' and its distance is 130 pcs. 

6. How many times larger than the Sun is Betelgeuse? That is, calculate its radius R as a multiple R= \# R. What is the value of \#?

## The Cosmic Distance Ladder

For objects within about 1 AU, distances were accurately determined using radio detection and ranging (RaDAR): We bounce radio-wavelength light off of Venus and wait to hear (see?) the echo to find that it is roughly 0.3 AU at closest approach. 

Stellar parallax is useful for measuring the distance to the nearest stars, but it is useless for anything further out, precisely because we are measuring the parallax relative to the stuff further out. To find the distance to the “fixed stars” we need to find the next rung in this **ladder of cosmic distance scales**. We will do this using the spectroscopic properties of stars, but to talk about those, we first need to discuss the brightness of stars and how it relates to distance.

### Luminosity and Radiant Flux

In Advanced Astronomy: Solar System (AA:SS), we verified the **luminosity** of the Sun to be L☉=3.831026 W and checked that at 1 AU, this gave a solar constant of F☉=1360 W/m2. The luminosity is a measure of “power” which is the amount of energy produced per unit time (a.k.a. energy production rate). The solar constant is, on the other hand, a measure of power crossing a surface per unit area. In this case, the relevant amount is how much impinges on our upper atmosphere:

solar constant \= luminosityarea=energyarea  time

The luminosity is an intrinsic property of a star. How bright the star appears to us is called the **apparent brightness**. (In the example of the Sun as seen from Earth, this apparent brightness is called the solar constant.) We see here that it is a quantity (energy) *per unit time and per unit area*. In physics, such a quantity is called a “flux”. In this case, the apparent brightness is an energy flux called the **radiant flux**. It is usually denoted by the letter F. 

7. The radiant flux is a function of both the luminosity of the star and the distance d from which it is being viewed. Check that at a distance of 1 AU, the quoted value of the solar constant is the one that follows from the quoted value of the luminosity. In particular, show that 

		F=L4d2

In AA:SS, we verified that the fusion of protons into helium in the p-p chain gives the correct solar luminosity, thereby explaining the energy-generation mechanism responsible for all life as we know it. What was not emphasized is that this calculation (and ones similar to it) imply that luminosities are intrinsic properties of stars: If you understand what type of star you are looking at, you can (in principle) calculate/know its luminosity. If you now measure its apparent brightness, you will be able to deduce the distance to that star\! This method is called **spectroscopic parallax**. It is useful for distance measurements out to \~10,000 pc.

Further on, we will see how the stellar spectra are used to determine a star’s luminosity. First, however, we need to familiarize ourselves with the system used by astronomers to give a precise determination of apparent brightness. 

### The Magnitude Scale

Astronomers use a byzantine measure to describe the apparent brightness of a star. Its origin’s lie in Hipparchus’s classification of stars using a scale from 1 to 6 with 6 being the faintest stars visible with the naked eye (which is all he had). The difference in energy flux between these extremes is a factor of roughly 100, so each step in Hipparchus’s scale corresponds to a decrease in brightness by a factor of 51002.5. 

8. The **magnitude scale** is the modern version of Hipparchus’s scale. Note that Hipparchus’ scale is *additive* where the brightness scale is *multiplicative*: a decrease in the flux by a *factor* of 2.5 increases the magnitude by *adding* 1\.  
   1. Using this fact and the discussion above, argue that the apparent magnitude m should be related to the radiant flux by F100-m5.   
   2. Use your result on the dependence of the flux on the distance d to argue that 100m5d2.   
   3. Take the log with base 10 to conclude that m5d. (Hint: First show that the result in part b. means that d10m5.)

Taking the logarithm of a dimensionful quantity (d has the dimensions of length) does not make sense. We fix this by introducing the following normalization: The **absolute magnitude** M is defined to be the apparent magnitude an object *would* have *were* you to move it to a distance of 10 pcs. Putting all this together, we arrive at the relation 

(1)		m-M \= 5  d10

between the apparent magnitude m, the absolute magnitude M, and the distance d measured in parsecs. The quantity m-M appearing on the LHS of (1) is a measure of distance called the distance modulus. 

9. The Sun’s apparent magnitude is m= \-26.7. Calculate its  
   1. distance modulus (Hint: You already know how far away it is, but make sure you are using the right units.)  
   2. absolute magnitude

   What do these numbers mean about the brightness of our Sun relative to the stars we see at night? How bright would our Sun appear to be if it were 10 pcs away?

   

10. The table below shows magnitude data for two famous stars and one less famous star. 

| Star | apparent magnitude *m* | Absolute magnitude *M* | Distance Modulus *m* \- *M* |
| :---- | ----- | ----- | ----- |
| Sirius | \-1.44 | 1.41 | \-2.85 |
| Betelgeuse | 0.45 | \-5.14 | 5.59 |
| GJ 75 | 5.63 | 5.63 | 0.00 |

   


    1. What is special about GJ 75?  
    2. Calculate the distance to Sirius and Betelgeuse in parsecs.  
    3. Rank these stars in terms of increasing luminosity.  
    4. Rank these stars in terms of increasing distance.   
    5. Which of these stars is the brightest and why?

## Spectral Classification

Recall that light passing through the gas and plasma of a star’s atmosphere will be absorbed in specific frequencies by the atoms and molecules in the gas and reradiated in random directions resulting in dark lines in the stellar spectrum. We have discussed the use of this fact to identify the trace elements present in the star, but we can use it also for the very important task of determining the star’s surface temperature.   
   
Consider the hydrogen lines in the spectra below. If you look carefully, you will see that those lines are darker in the alphabetical progression A, B, …, F, G, …, K, …, M, …, O. This progression was how astronomers organized the spectral diagrams of stars before the quantum mechanical revolution. 

## ![][image3]

The advent of the quantum model of the atom gave a better method to organize the spectra and explained why they looked different. Contrary to expectations, the difference has little to do with chemical composition, and everything to do with temperature. The so-called “Harvard spectral classification” reorders the letters in terms of decreasing temperature to OBAFGKM. 

11. The Balmer lines of the hydrogen atom correspond to energy transitions between the first excited state n=2 and all other states n=3,4, .... The energy of these transitions are given by the formula   
      
    En \= 13.6 eV 1n2.   
      
    To be able to absorb visible light, the electrons absorbing it must be in the first excited state with energy E2. In the ground state, electrons have energy E1 so to get to the first excited state needed for the Balmer lines, an energy E=E1-E2is needed.  Convert the energy E to temperature and compare it to the temperatures in the OBAFGKM classification. Does your answer make sense to you?   
    

The temperature you just computed is far too high to explain the strength of the Balmer lines in stellar spectra. As we discussed in class, that is because in the correct way of doing this calculation, we need to determine the average number of atoms in the first excited state from a statistical analysis similar to the one that gives rise to the Maxwell speed distribution. 

The equation that determines the ratio of atoms in successive states is called the [Saha equation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Saha_ionization_equation). It is of the form 

R(T)=1bT32e-EkBT 

where T is the temperature in Kelvin and E energy difference between the states (in Kelvin). The coefficient b is some numerical factor of order 1\. To get the fraction of atoms in the first excited state, we need the ratio of atoms in the first excited state to all the atoms in the system. For reasons I am happy to explain, this works out to the combination 

f(T)=R(T)1+R(T)

12. Using the value of E you found in problem 11, plot f(T) in Desmos (or some other graphing application). Hint: Adjust the axes of the plot by clicking on the wrench symbol and setting the T-axis to run from 4,000 K to 25,000 K and the vertical axis to run from \-1 to 2 (this is a fraction after all). When you define the function R, it should ask whether you want a slider for b. Say yes and set it to run between 0.1 and 10\.

13. Play with the slider to see what happens with the curve. Set the slider to b5. What is the value of the temperature for at which half of the atoms are in the first excited state?

## 

## Stellar Parameters from the Planck Spectrum

When we studied the radiation spectrum of the Sun, we discussed the Planck curve for a perfect emitter (a.k.a. a blackbody):

(2)		u()3eb-1		(Planck)	  
This function describes the amount of radiation a blackbody emits as a function of frequency. Here b=kBT is, up to two fundamental constants, the reciprocal of the temperature of the blackbody. In particular, we see that (up to irrelevant constants) the blackbody curve is determined entirely by the temperature, with the peak moving up and to the right (toward higher frequency) with increasing temperature (decreasing b). 

The position of the peak corresponds to a particular wavelength 

(3)		peak=aT		(Wien’s “Law”)

This relation is called “Wien’s displacement law”. The constant a=2.9010-3 mK is called Wien’s displacement constant. Note that if you measure T in Kelvin, then b3 mm.[^2]

The area under the curve can be calculated from calculus. It represents the total energy radiated by the blackbody. In terms of the radiant flux, the result is called the Stefan-Boltzmann Law:

(4)		F= T4		where 		\=2kB4603c2	(Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

is called the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

14. Show that \=5.67 10-8 Wm2  K4. Does this give the right units for the radiant flux?  
15. Recalling the relation between stellar luminosity L and distance, use the Stefan-Boltzmann law to show that the surface temperature T, luminosity L, and stellar radius R are related by

(5)		L=4 R2 T4 

16. Use the new form of the Stefan-Bolzmann law to verify that the surface temperature of the Sun is T☉=5800 K. (The radius of the sun is R☉=6.96108 m and the luminosity was discussed above.)

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law in the form (5) is useful for finding stellar sizes from their luminosities and temperatures. Often, we would like to know the sizes and luminosities in terms of solar luminosity L☉and solar radius R☉, but the temperature in Kelvin. When this is the case, the formula (5) simplifies to 

(5\*)		L=R2 T58004			(in solar luminosity, solar radius, and Kelvin ) 

## 

## Spectroscopic Parallax

To determine the distances to stars, we can use the **Hertzsprung-Russell diagram**. For example, since most stars (90%) are main sequence stars, we can determine their magnitude if we know their spectral type because they lie on the main sequence line. Of course, if a star happens to be a supergiant, this technique will be way off. 

Hertzsprung thought of a way to do better: He realized that by looking more carefully at the spread of the spectral lines, he could determine the density of the stellar atmosphere. 

17. Calculate the mass density of the Sun. Compare your result to the density of water. Then calculate the mass density of Betelgeuse assuming M=10M and R=1000 R.  
18. Calculate the surface gravity (recall g=GMR2) of the Sun and of Betelgeuse.

Hertzsprung realized that dense atmospheres cause widening in the spectral lines. This effect is now called **pressure broadening**. This happens because in dense atmospheres, collisions of atoms in the process of undergoing an emission, can collide inelastically with another atom. When this happens, the other atom will carry away some energy which must, therefore, be surrendered by the escaping photon. This shifts the wavelength of the photon, thereby broadening the line. This gives a refinement of the spectral data in which the size of the star can be determined called the **luminosity class** of the star. It is a roman numeral from I – V starting from very luminous supergiants (I\&II) down to main sequence dwarves (V). (See table 17.3 in the text.) Thus, the full spectral classification of the Sun is G2V: The “G2” part indicates that it has a temperature of 5800 K and the “V” means that it is a main sequence star. It is called a “yellow dwarf”. (It is actually white, but the term “white dwarf refers to a type of stellar remnant, as we will discuss later.) Betelgeuse has stellar classification M1Ia corresponding to a surface temperature around 3600 K (“M1”) and it is a luminous supergiant (“Ia”).[^3] 

![][image4]

|  | Alpha Centauri A | Barnard | Sol | Sirius A | Aldebaran | Antares | Betelgeuse |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| T (K) |  |  | 6000 |  |  |  |  |
| R (R) |  |  | 1 |  |  |  |  |
| L (L) |  |  | 1 |  |  |  |  |
| m | \- 0.01 | 9.5 | \- 26.7 | \- 1.46 | 0.75 | 0.6 | 0.0 |
| MV |  |  | 4.83 |  |  |  |  |
| spectral type | G2 | M4 | G2 | A0 | K5 | M1 | M1 |
| luminosity class | V | V | V | V | III | Ib | Ia |
| d |  |  | 0 pc | 2.7 pc | 20 pc | 170 pc | ? |

19. Fill out the table above. In this table, the temperature T is in Kelvin and the distance d is the distance to the Sun in parsecs. In this exercise use the various relations you know between size, type, temperature, distance, etc… You are not aiming for precision so it’s OK if you cannot determine certain numbers exactly from the information given.  
    

There are competing conventions for magnitude and luminosity too numerous to list here. However, for the purposes of the table above, let’s work out the conversion from absolute (visual) magnitude to luminosity. According to the discussion surrounding formula (1) above, the magnitude is proportional to the log of the luminosity

	M= \-5LL0	so	L= L0100- M5

The constant luminosity L0 is called the zero-point luminosity. It is fixed by convention to the value L0=3.01 1028 W, but if we work in multiples of the solar luminosity, we find   
L= L 100M-M5

where M=4.83 is the absolute solar magnitude. 

[^1]:  If you didn’t take AA: SS, you probably do not know what this means, so call me over. It is my responsibility to make sure you have everything you need to succeed in this course, but it is your responsibility to reach out, because I will forget to explain things. 

[^2]: This “law” is not a fundamental result, but it is a useful heuristic. To really determine the temperature of a star, the entire Planck curve is used. In practice, the spectrum is filtered through two different color filters giving magnitudes at two different frequencies. This determines the Planck curve, and therefore the temperature, unambiguously. 

[^3]:  Remember that terms like “yellow” and “red” are only meant to be suggestive of the spectral type. In fact red giants emit most of their light in the infrared whereas “blue” stars emit mostly in the ultraviolet.
